than just the activities. When a newsletter, so the newsletter industry evolved to where it was controlled by a number of copywriters who were very highly paid and it was incestuous. I mean, because it was basically a very closed business where they were renting each other's lists. It's not unlike the online market, but it was even more incestuous. Dan would rent my mailing list to sell his newsletter. I'd met yours, you'd met Dan's. And pray anybody who had new, fresh names, everyone would pounds on him. It was all direct mail generated. So I did about four things that are pretty cool. When a mailing piece stopped working that somebody had spent a lot of money on and a writer would spend a lot on, I would repurpose it. I would find like, for example, a newsletter wanted to break even after I taught him, many of them wanted to still make a lot of money. Well, let's say you had a gold newsletter and you're at break even, you don't want to mail anymore. I'd go to a gold brokerage firm, because most of the newsletter promotions were based on a premium or a report. And the report was still good, unless the economics changed. I would buy the rights on a performance to that piece. I'd use it for a client that I would get who is a gold dealer or a rare coin dealer or a gold stock dealer or a penny stock dealer because they're used to losing their ass on the acquisition. Then we'd either try to first see if we could break even or lose a little bit selling the newsletter and keep all the money, or at least everything, but the $6 hard cost. If we couldn't, we would move it over to a lead generator. So that's the first thing. The second thing I did, and this made me $8 million personally one year, and I wish I'd kept doing it. This is 25 years ago. When I started, newsletters were all that the people sent out. They would play act at ancillary sales and they'd stick in the letter, a one page, eight and a half by 11 or a sheet with an ad, a little ad on one side and it would pull three, four grand and so nobody thought there was any viability there. But they were sending these newsletters out second class and they could absorb a lot more weight. Well, I looked at the industry and thought, okay, Dan's mailing my list and he's happy to break even, but sometimes he makes triple on my list and it was about, as I recall, 65 or 70 cents a piece in the mail with the list rental. I thought, well, if I could take Dan's mailing piece and stick it in the newsletter for 60 cents less, it's gonna do as well and maybe even if it's not endorsed, it's gonna do better because it has the implication of endorsement. I thought, hmm, so even if it broke even, if I only gave Dan hard cost and I got the spread, I could make a lot of money. So I went out and I played with it and I thought, well, I don't see what's wrong with this. So I went out and bought the rights to do inserts in about 20 newsletters. Then after I tied the inserts up, I had the rights to do an up to eight page insert in an eight or 16 page newsletter. Now, it's like an eight or 16 page newsletter Dan's sending had nothing in the middle. I got the right to stick something in the middle and if there was an incremental cost, I paid it but because of the weight factors, there was rarely. Then all I did was I'd go to all the same people that were renting Dan's list. I would take their mailing pieces and I would make them better. I come up with a better headline, better risk reversal, better bonus, a more preferential pricing so it looked to the consumer realistically like they were getting a greater value from the relationship. I would take 16 pages and you thought that was bad. Put it down to seven point but use color so it would at least, it would make it readable and stick it inside the newsletter. And the first time I did it, I made a half a million. I could never, I'll never forget it. I had a little, the house, I had a beautiful little house overlooking the ocean. I had a little office outside and the post guy comes and he had four canvas bags. You remember those days? Each one had a five and I was the Aidan sisters. You remember the Aidan sisters? It had the eight, because I was selling the Aidan sisters for Jim Blanchard, but I did that and it was very successful. I did early renewal, I did reactivations and it was pretty cool what I did. So you have a subscriber. The subscriber no longer wants to subscribe to you. So you lost him or her. So I had to come up with innovative ways to reactivate him and the most innovative way I did, and it was pretty ballsy, I would go to five or six competitive newsletters. And I'd say, Dan, I'm gonna sell your newsletter and I'm gonna give you hard costs. Hard cost back then was six bucks, but his newsletter may be 300. Then I'd write a letter to Howard and Ruff's expires and I'd say, obviously we let you down. You don't know what we've been doing lately. We think we're a greater investment than ever and we wanna put our self on the line and do something so ballsy no one else would. If you'll give us a second chance, we'll buy you a full term subscription to any of our competitors. And then judges, if we don't deliver, you can get a pro rata, because our risk was six bucks and we're getting back two or three years in most of them stayed. I mean, I did just tons of things like that, but I've done, it's basically looking at things very non-linear, sort of connecting.
